item.content:encoded: Pyrex And Anchor Hocking Look Similar, But They’re Not The Same—Here’s Why A professional chef breaks down the differences between these beloved glassware brands. By Kait Hanson Kait Hanson Kait Hanson is a lifestyle reporter with more than ten years of experience writing stories on entertainment, travel, food and more. Her work has been featured by Vanity Fair, CNN, TIME, TODAY and more. Southern Living's editorial guidelines Published on July 09, 2026 In This Article View All In This Article Similarities Types Of Glass Pyrex Anchor Hocking Which Is Better? When trying to pick out quality glass cookware for your kitchen, brands like Pyrex and Anchor Hocking may be top of mind. Both brands have made a name for themselves in the durable glass cookware market and come with glowing reviews from loyal fans. It begs the question: What is the difference between Pyrex vs. Anchor Hocking? How do the two brands compare? Francine Marz, a South Carolina chef, breaks it down for us. Credit: Caitlin Bensel What’s Similar About Pyrex Vs. Anchor Hocking? Marz tells Southern Living the brands are similar in what they create: glass cookware, bakeware, and kitchen accessories. “Both are made of the same kind of materials, both companies are over 100 years old, both offer limited warranties on their products, and both companies offer a wide variety of items,” she says. “Both are BPA-free and depending on your stance, that could equate to another positive attribute of using glass cooking (and) baking vessels.” Plus, both brands create products using soda-lime glass and can be put into an oven up to 425 degrees Fahrenheit. Y’all Don’t Want To Miss This Join Southern Living Insiders today to unlock 60 years of vintage recipes, exclusive deals, a special edition of our best-selling cookbook, and a weekly newsletter just for Insiders. Types Of Glass Oven-safe cookware like Pyrex and Anchor Hocking are made with unique glass that’s resistant to heat, chemicals, and electricity. They are safe to use in the oven and the freezer. There are three types of this glassware: soda-lime, borosilicate, and tempered. Borosilicate glass is made of boron oxide and silica. As cookware, it is extremely durable, and it is also used in making glass laboratory supplies. Soda-lime glass is made from a mixture of soda, lime, and silica. It has a lower resistance to heat and chemicals than borosilicate glass. Tempering makes glass stronger and more resistant to thermal shock. What Is Pyrex? Founded in New York in 1915, Pyrex was born in New York when Bessie Littleton was seeking a solution to a cracked baking dish. At the time, her husband was working for Corning Glass Works and provided Bessie with a sawed-off glass jar to test for baking. It worked—and the rest is history. Pyrex produces glass bakeware and cookware, glass storage containers, and the iconic glass measuring cups many of us have in our own kitchens. Early Pyrex was made from borosilicate, which could withstand extreme temperature changes, or thermal shock, without shattering. The company switched to tempered soda-lime glass in the 1950s to keep the cost affordable for consumers while still providing glassware tough enough for everyday cooking. It is stronger than borosilicate, making it less likely to shatter when dropped. It is also less heat resistant than vintage Pyrex made with borosilicate. Vintage Pyrex is marked as “PYREX,” in all capital letters. Today, the company’s glassware is labeled as “pyrex.” What Is Anchor Hocking? Anchor Hocking was founded in 1905 by Isaac Jacob Collins in Ohio. Collins’ goal was simple: to make glass bakeware that was accessible. More than 100 years later, the brand has evolved from glass bakeware into other glass products that include food storage, serveware, drinkware, mixing bowls, and more. Anchor Hocking glass bakeware is made with tempered soda-lime glass, giving it durability and heat resistance. Like Pyrex, it was originally made with borosilicate glass. Is Glass Good For Baking? Both brands produce glass bakeware and cookware, but is glass the best option when it comes to cooking and baking? Marz says glassware in the kitchen is beloved because it typically bakes evenly every time. Glassware retains heat to keep food warm longer, it doesn’t stain, and you can bake, serve, and store all in one dish. “I think it’s fabulous, but it is heavy and it sometimes can be hard to carry around or move,” she says. “It doesn’t rust like metal and it’s easy to clean; however, you have to be careful to make sure that you don’t expose the bakeware to sudden extreme heat changes.” How To Safely Use Pyrex And Anchor Hocking Soda-lime glass, which is used to make both Pyrex and Anchor Hocking glassware, is safe to use in the oven and the freezer. But there are a few guidelines to prevent shattering. Avoid Extreme Temperatures Soda-lime glassware is less thermal resistant than earlier versions made with borosilicate, so don’t subject it to extreme temperatures, such as going straight from the freezer to the hot oven. Bring a casserole frozen in a Pyrex or Anchor Hocking dish to room temperature before putting it in the oven. And a hot dish should be allowed to cool completely before storing it in the freezer. Don’t Use The Cooktop This type of glassware is not suitable for stovetop cooking. You also shouldn’t set it on a hot cooktop when placing or removing it from the oven. Let It Cool When removing glass bakeware from the oven, let it cool on a wire baking rack instead of on a cool surface like the counter. Make sure the surface is dry and not wet to prevent cracking. A hot glass dish should also cool before washing or storing in the refrigerator or freezer. Check For Chips Periodically check your glass cookware for chips and cracks. These can weaken the dish and make it more prone to breakage in a hot oven or due to changes in temperature. Make Sure It’s Oven-Safe Before putting glass baking dishes into the oven, microwave, freezer, or dishwasher, check the bottom of the dish or contact the manufacturer to see if it’s safe to do so. Which Is Better: Pyrex Or Anchor Hocking? So, which brand takes gold? Marz says that answer is subjective. “I really think it is personal preference and we tend to use what our parents used in the kitchen,” Marz says. “The rituals of baking and cooking stimulate memories, feelings, and emotions, and something as simple as a glass baking dish might not mean anything to one person, but could mean so much to another. Today there are so many knockoff brands that one has to truly be loyal to a brand to keep purchasing it.” Explore more: Food and Recipes Kitchen Assistant Was this page helpful? Thanks for your feedback! Tell us why! Other Submit

item.content:encoded: Consumer Reports Tested 52 Rice Products. These Had the Lowest Arsenic Levels Researchers detected inorganic arsenic in every rice product they tested, underscoring concerns first raised more than a decade ago. By Korin Miller Korin Miller Korin Miller has spent nearly two decades covering food, health, and nutrition for digital, print, and TV platforms. Her work has appeared in Women's Health, SELF, Prevention, The Washington Post, and more. Food & Wine's Editorial Guidelines Published on July 8, 2026 In This Article View All In This Article Consumer Reports found arsenic in every rice product it tested How does arsenic end up in rice? Should you worry about arsenic in rice? How to reduce your exposure to arsenic in rice Rice products with the highest (and lowest) arsenic levels, tested by Consumer Reports Credit: InnaFelker / Getty Images A new Consumer Reports analysis found inorganic arsenic in every rice product it tested, with brown rice averaging higher levels than white rice. Basmati and sushi rice had lower average inorganic arsenic levels than other rice types tested. Rice absorbs more inorganic arsenic than most other grains because it is typically grown in flooded fields, where the element is more readily taken up from soil and water. Experts say the findings aren’t a reason to panic, but choosing lower-arsenic rice varieties, rotating in other grains, and cooking rice in excess water may help reduce exposure to inorganic arsenic. Arsenic has been behind a slew of food recalls over the past few years, from apple juice to supplements. But the highly toxic chemical element has repeatedly surfaced in rice. According to a new Consumer Reports report, the problem isn’t getting better. For the report, researchers tested 52 rice products for arsenic and detected inorganic levels of the compound in all of them. Consumer Reports originally tested rice for arsenic in 2012 and followed up on that testing in 2014. “Consumer Reports has long warned that rice absorbs significantly more inorganic arsenic from soil and water than other crops,” says James Rogers, PhD, Consumer Reports’ director of food safety. Arsenic is a naturally occurring compound found in air, water, and soil, according to the National Cancer Institute (NCI). It can also be released into the environment through agricultural and industrial activities. Arsenic comes in two forms — organic and inorganic. The inorganic form is more toxic and is linked to an increased risk of bladder and skin cancers, among other cancers, according to the NCI. But experts stress that the problem of arsenic in rice is a complicated one. Here’s how concerned you should be about the findings, plus how to lower your risk. Consumer Reports found arsenic in every rice product it tested For the report, researchers analyzed total and inorganic arsenic levels in two to three samples of 52 rice products — arborio, basmati, brown, jasmine, sushi, white, and precooked microwave rice, as well as rice side dishes that use seasoning. The products were also tested for cadmium, lead, and mercury. Based on the findings, the researchers calculated the number of servings adults and children can safely have of each product per week. (Servings varied slightly by product, but typically meant ¼ cup of dry rice.) Billions Eat This Staple Grain Daily, But Climate Change Could Poison the World’s Supply Overall, the researchers found detectable levels of arsenic, cadmium, and mercury in all samples, and lead in 66 samples. Although there is no federal limit for inorganic arsenic in rice, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has set a limit of 100 parts per billion for infant rice cereal. Brown rice consistently contained more inorganic arsenic than white rice, averaging 113 parts per billion compared to an average of 72 parts per billion. “Basmati rice and sushi rice averaged less than other rice types,” Rogers says. Every product had slightly different results, but some of the biggest sources of inorganic arsenic included Ben’s Original Long Grain & Wild Rice (167 parts per billion), Mahatma Jasmine Brown Thai Fragrant Whole Rice (155 PPB), and Goya Organics Long Grain Brown Rice (150 PPB). While brand wasn’t necessarily correlated with high levels of arsenic — some of the brands just mentioned also had rice with lower levels of arsenic — Lundberg consistently had some of the lowest levels of arsenic in its rice products. How does arsenic end up in rice? It’s important to note that companies aren’t adding arsenic to their rice products. Instead, the compound can enter rice during the growing process. “Rice has a unique biology that makes this a difficult problem to solve,” says Scott Keatley, RD, co-owner of Keatley Medical Nutrition Therapy in New York City. “It naturally accumulates arsenic more readily than most other grains because it’s typically grown in flooded fields, where arsenic becomes more available for uptake by the plant.” A New Study Says Up to 17% of the World's Cropland Could Be Contaminated With Toxic Metals Contaminated soil and water allow arsenic to be incorporated into rice plants during the growing process, leading to arsenic contamination of the rice people eat, explains Kelly Johnson-Arbor, MD, a toxicologist at MedStar Health. “In order to significantly reduce this type of contamination, rice growers and processors must change their practices,” she says. “This might include growing rice in drier conditions — which results in less exposure to contaminated water during the growing process — or developing genetically modified strains of rice that are less likely to absorb arsenic from soil or water.” But these types of interventions take years to develop, research, and implement, which could explain why arsenic contamination in rice hasn’t declined over the last decade, Johnson-Arbor says. Should you worry about arsenic in rice? The primary concern is long-term exposure, according to Jamie Alan, PhD, an associate professor of pharmacology and toxicology at Michigan State University. “This is inorganic arsenic, which compared to organic arsenic, is much more toxic,” she says. It’s difficult to know exactly how concerning the findings are, given that there is no upper limit on arsenic exposure for adults and children, she says. “Generally, adults and children can tolerate more of external toxins like arsenic than infants, so I would expect that these groups could safely consume more,” Alan says. “However, I cannot be sure because the data does not exist.” But Johnson-Arbor says that a lot depends on your overall diet. “Plant-based foods can contain detectable levels of heavy metals, including arsenic, cadmium, and lead,” she says. “However, for people who eat a balanced diet that contains various types of foods, it is unlikely that such exposures will result in toxicity or unwanted health effects.” How to reduce your exposure to arsenic in rice Levels of inorganic arsenic in rice depend in part on where it is grown. “Studies have generally found lower inorganic arsenic levels in rice grown in places like California, India, Pakistan, and Thailand, while higher levels are more common in rice from parts of the south-central United States — particularly Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, and Texas — because flooded growing conditions and local soil chemistry increase arsenic uptake,” Keatley says. “For the average person, that means the risk depends not just on how much rice you eat, but also where it was grown.” Keatley also recommends rotating other whole grains into your diet beyond rice, such as quinoa, barley, farro, oats, and sorghum, to reduce cumulative exposure while maintaining nutritional variety. “When possible, choose lower-arsenic varieties like basmati, and consider cooking rice in excess water and draining it afterward, which can substantially reduce its inorganic arsenic content,” he says. Consumer Reports tested 52 rice products and found detectable inorganic arsenic in every sample, echoing concerns first raised more than a decade ago. The toxic form of arsenic has been linked to cancer with long-term exposure. sakai000 / Getty Images If you’re concerned about arsenic in rice, Johnson-Arbor suggests limiting your rice intake to a few servings a week and avoiding prepackaged rice with spices, which tends to have consistently higher arsenic levels. Overall, Alan says the findings aren’t a reason to panic, but they do suggest people should be mindful of the type of rice they eat. “If you can switch your brand of rice to one with lower levels, then do so,” she says. “Our bodies are equipped to handle a certain amount of external toxins. If you have rice once or twice a week, you are completely fine.” Rice products with the highest (and lowest) arsenic levels, tested by Consumer Reports The amount of inorganic arsenic varied widely depending on the type of rice and the specific product tested. Brown rice generally had higher arsenic levels than white rice, while basmati and sushi rice tended to have lower levels overall. Consumer Reports also calculated how many servings per week adults and children could safely eat of each product based on its findings. The tables below break down the arsenic levels and the recommended weekly serving limits for every rice product included in the testing. Use them to compare brands and identify products that may help reduce your overall arsenic exposure. Arsenic levels in brown rice BRAND ARSENIC LEVELS (PPB) MAX WEEKLY SERVINGS (ADULTS) MAX WEEKLY SERVINGS (CHILDREN) Mahatma Jasmine Brown Thai Fragrant Whole Rice 155 2.25 0.5 Goya Organics Long Grain WEEKLY SERVINGS (CHILDREN) Seeds of Change 90 Second Organic Jasmine Rice 40 2.5 0.75 Great Value 90 Second Long Grain White Rice 37 3 0.75 Ben’s Original Ready Rice Long Grain White 37 3 0.75 Mahatma Near East Rice Pilaf Spanish Rice 144 1.5 0.5 Rice-A-Roni Cilantro Lime 127 1.75 0.5 Lundberg Organic Cilantro Lime Rice 105 2.25 0.5 Goya Spanish Style Yellow Rice 100 3.5 0.75 Zatarain’s Yellow Rice 96 2.75 0.75 Explore more: News Health News Was this page helpful?
